instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
What Is the Difference Between Active and Passive Investing?
When formulating an investment plan, an essential aspect to consider is whether you want to park your money in active funds or passive funds. For the uninitiated, there are two broad ways of categorising investments in the equity market: active investing and passive investing. These two types denote little difference in the kinds of stocks a fund manager would choose. Rather, the major difference lies mainly in the investment strategy and the manner of investing practised by the fund manager.
The returns you receive on your investment depend on the kind of strategy chosen by the fund manager. Hence, having a clear understanding of each type of investment – both active and passive investing - is crucial to selecting the right market-linked insurance plan and meeting your financial goals efficiently.
To help you pick the most suitable option, we list have compared active investing vs passive investing.
What is active investing?
Active investing involves a fund manager/investment manager being actively involved in the stocks that are a part of the investment or market-linked life insurance plan (such as a ULIP). The fund manager operates with the intention to choose the ideal stocks for the plan to maximise returns and minimise risks for the investor. The investment is carried out with the aim to beat the benchmark.
In addition to choosing which stocks to add and which to remove, the fund manager also decides the concentration of different kinds of stocks in the plan in active investing. Essentially, a fund manager has a larger control over the fund’s performance.
When selecting a fund, you will get a disclosure on whether the fund follows the active investing method. Most likely, you will also receive information on the fund manager, since they play a pivotal role in the process.
What is passive investing?
In the passive investment strategy, the fund manager has little to no control over how the fund is managed. There is not much requirement of it either, since the funds of a passive investment plan are not expected to beat the benchmark. Rather, the fund follows the trajectory of a particular index and ensures that the fund tracks the index correctly.
Hence, it is the insurance company or the AMC (Asset Management Company) that handles the fund. Even when a fund manager is present in passive investing, their roles are mostly limited to ensuring that the fund is in line with the index in regard to changing laws and policy constraints.
Though index funds constitute a large portion of passive investing, there are other kinds of passive funds as well. The insurance company or the AMC must disclose whether the investment plan you are choosing follows the passive strategy.
Difference between active investing and passive investing
After going through the basics, let’s understand the differences that arise after pitting active vs passive investing:
Risk factor
Risks are more prevalent in an actively managed fund rather than in a passively managed fund. Since the fund manager is trying to beat the benchmark, they may choose some stocks that have a higher risk factor. The risk factor is high with an active ULIP also, because there are constant decisions and movements being implemented.
In comparison, the risks inherent in a passive fund are minimal.
Returns
The returns of an actively managed fund are higher than that of a passively managed fund. This is because the fund manager is consistently moving around stocks in an attempt to beat the benchmark. An investment calculator can give you an estimate of the returns you may expect with different kinds of funds.
However, the returns of both funds may differ depending on how you look at them. In a short-term market, the returns of an active fund may be higher. But a long-term passive fund can outperform an active fund in the long run.
Expenses incurred
While the expense ratio is present in both types of investment plans - active and passive - it is likely to be higher in an actively managed fund. This is due to the work involved in such a fund. In passive investing, there is lesser labour involved in the management of the fund, and thus, the expenses charged by the insurance company may be considerably lesser.
For instance, the expense ratio of most index funds is around 0.5% while most types of active investment funds have an expense ratio of around 2-2.25%.
Now, which one should you pick?
As you may have observed, after contrasting active vs passive investing, pros and cons can be observed. Which type of ULIP would be the ideal plan for you depends on several subjective factors unique to each investor, such as their investment goals, risk appetite, expense budget, and so on.
Also, one must remember that investing in the market, especially in equities, is fraught with risk. However, one can minimise this risk and maximise their returns by being careful with picking the right funds, using the investment calculator, and following the advice of experts.
Knowing the differences between active investing vs passive investing can help you in making the right additions to your investment portfolio. Hence, ensure to use the information presented here correctly to meet your goals and secure your financial future.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article is not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale.

output.final_text:
What Is the Difference Between Active and
Passive Investing? There are various ways of investing in the market and gaining returns as per market performance. Opting for a Unit-Linked Insurance Plan or a ULIP may be one such effective way to invest in the market and gain financial security alongside. Whether you may be investing via the conventional route of a retail investment plan or through a market-linked insurance plan, such as a ULIP, an essential aspect to consider may be whether you want to park your money in active funds or passive funds. For the uninitiated, there are two broad ways of categorising investments in the equity market: active investing and passive investing. The major difference between the two lies mainly in the investment strategy and the manner of investing practised by the fund manager. [1] The returns you may receive on your investment may depend on the kind of strategy chosen by the fund manager, among other factors. Hence, having a clear understanding of each type of investment – both active and passive investing – may be crucial to selecting the right market-linked insurance plan and meeting your financial goals efficiently. To help you pick the most suitable option, we have compared active investing vs passive investing. What is active investing? [2][6][4] Active investing involves a fund manager/investment manager being actively involved in the stocks that are a part of the investment plan or market-linked life insurance policy. The fund manager usually operates with the intention to choose the appropriate stocks for the plan to maximise returns and minimise risks for the investor. The fund manager selects a benchmark index which is a standard against which the performance of a security, investment strategy, or investment manager can be measured. The investment may be carried out with the aim to surpass the benchmark returns of the index in which the selected stocks are listed. In addition to choosing which stocks to add and which to remove, the fund manager may also decide the concentration of different kinds of stocks in the plan in active investing.
What is passive investing? [2] In the passive investment strategy, the fund manager may have little to no control over how the fund is managed. The funds of a passive investment plan may not ideally be expected to beat the benchmark. Rather, the fund tends to follow the trajectory of a particular index and ensures that the fund tracks the index correctly. [3] The role of a fund manager in a passive investing strategy may be mostly limited to ensuring that the fund is in line with changes in selected benchmark index and plan’s terms and conditions. [4] Though index funds constitute a large portion of passive investing, there are other kinds of passive funds as well. [5] Difference between active investing and passive investing After going through the basics, let’s understand the differences that arise after pitting active vs passive investing:
 Risk factor Any investment made in the equity market are likely have an element of risk attached to it. The performance of your funds may depend on a host of factors, many of which may not be within your control, regardless of the investment strategy you opt for. Nevertheless, active funds may have a higher element of risk since the aim of the strategy is to usually surpass the benchmark. To achieve the aim, the fund manager may choose some stocks that have a higher risk element. The risk factor may be high with an active ULIP also, because there may be constant decisions and movements being implemented. Though passive funds may often be considered to have lower risks6, there is one thing you still have to keep in mind, is that the risks may increase depending upon the market situation. At times, passive funds may be more adversely affected than active funds.
 Returns An active investment plan may have better returns since its objective is to usually surpass the benchmark returns of the index. Passive funds may only aim to track the index rather than surpass it, and so, the returns here may be lower. However, one may remember that there may be multiple factors that may have a positive or negative impact on the fund performance, whether the fund manager is following an active strategy or a passive strategy.
One may also look at the past performance of a fund to get a better idea; however, it may not be indicative of future yields, so one should ideally proceed with care. An investment calculator can give you an estimate of the returns you may expect with different kinds of funds. Now, which one should you pick? As you may have observed, after contrasting active vs passive investing, pros and cons may be observed. Which type of investment plan would be suitable for you depends on several subjective factors unique to each investor, such as their investment goals, risk appetite, expense budget, and so on. Hence, before opting for an active or passive ULIP or retail investment plan, one may consider these factors and proceed accordingly. Also, one should remember that investing in the market, especially in equities, is fraught with risk. However, one may minimise this risk and maximise their returns by being careful with picking the suitable funds, using the investment calculator, and following the advice of experts. Knowing the differences between active investing vs passive investing may help you in making the right additions to your investment portfolio. Hence, ensure to use the information presented here correctly to meet your goals and secure your financial future.
